about.title: About Us
about.subtitle: Helping travellers explore Kotor and the bay since 2007.
about.seoDesc: 15+ years helping visitors navigate the Bay of Kotor. Local heritage knowledge, Tivat Airport meet-and-greet, and honest advice on fortress hikes and bay roads.
about.p1: Since 2007, Kotor Car Hire has been connecting travellers with reliable, affordable rental cars in the Bay of Kotor. What started as a simple idea — making it easier for visitors to explore this UNESCO-listed corner of the Adriatic — has grown into one of the region's most trusted car rental services.
about.p2: The Bay of Kotor is best experienced at your own pace. The winding bay road, hidden mountain villages, and medieval towns are difficult to reach by public transport. We saw an opportunity to give travellers the freedom to discover Kotor and its surroundings on their own terms — no tour bus schedules, no missed viewpoints, no compromises.
about.p3a: We provide car rental services throughout
about.p3d: , and locations across the coast and interior.
about.promise1: Full insurance with zero excess options available
about.promise2: Tivat Airport meet-and-greet — we bring the car to you at arrivals
about.promise3: No hidden fees — the price you see is the price you pay
about.promise4: Cross-border travel permitted to Croatia, Bosnia, Albania, Serbia, and Kosovo
about.localText: Based in Kotor, we know every road around the bay — the best routes, the hidden gems, and the shortcuts that guidebooks miss. Whether you're driving from
about.localMid2: UNESCO Old Town, taking the scenic bay road to
about.localEnd: , or heading up the serpentine to Lovcen National Park, we can help you plan the perfect road trip.
about.contactText: Have a question about renting a car in Kotor? We're available via phone, WhatsApp, and Viber. Search our fleet online and book in minutes — or reach out and we'll help you find the perfect car for your trip.

kotor.title: Car Hire in Kotor
kotor.subtitle: Explore Montenegro's UNESCO jewel and the Bay of Kotor at your own pace.
kotor.seoDesc: Inside the medieval walls: Cat Museum, St Tryphon Cathedral, and 1,200 steps to San Giovanni fortress. Park outside the gates and explore on foot.
kotor.p1: Kotor is one of the best-preserved medieval towns on the Adriatic, sitting at the head of a dramatic fjord-like bay. The UNESCO-listed Old Town is a maze of narrow streets, Venetian palaces, Romanesque churches, and lively squares. Above it all, the fortress of San Giovanni climbs 1,200 steps to offer sweeping views across the bay.
kotor.p2b: gives you the freedom to explore the entire bay and beyond. Tivat Airport is just 8 km away — a 15-minute drive. Parking is available outside the Old Town walls, with larger lots on the approach roads.
kotor.stJohnText: The fortifications above Kotor date back to the 6th century and were expanded by the Venetians. The 1,200-step climb to the top rewards with panoramic views of the bay, Old Town rooftops, and the surrounding mountains. Entry is free outside peak season.
kotor.cathedralText: Built in 809 AD and reconstructed in 1124, the Cathedral of St. Tryphon is one of the finest Romanesque buildings on the Adriatic coast. The twin bell towers are Kotor's most recognisable landmark, and the interior houses a collection of medieval gold and silverwork.
kotor.ladyOfRocksTitle: Our Lady of the Rocks, Perast
kotor.ladyOfRocksText2: , this church sits on a man-made island in the Bay of Kotor. Since 1452, locals have thrown rocks into the bay to enlarge the island. Ferry boats run from Perast harbour for a few euros. The drive from Kotor takes 15 minutes along the bay road.
kotor.cetinjeText: Built in 1701, the monastery houses relics including the hand of John the Baptist. The drive from Kotor takes about an hour through the scenic mountain road via Njegos Mausoleum and Lovcen National Park.
kotor.royalCourtText: Montenegro's royal palace from 1871, located in the former royal capital. Displays art, artefacts, and photographs from the country's royal history.
kotor.routeDifficulty: Moderate — narrow streets in Old Town, standard roads approaching
kotor.routeVehicle: Compact car (easier parking near Old Town walls)
kotor.routeParking: Limited in Old Town. Use car parks on approach roads (€1-2/hour)
kotor.routeDistances: From Tivat Airport: 15 minutes · From Budva: 30 minutes

tivat-airport.subtitle: Collect your rental car at arrivals — just 8 km from Kotor Old Town.
tivat-airport.seoDesc: Land at TIV and be in Kotor's Old Town in 15 minutes. We meet every flight at arrivals — no queues, no shuttle buses. 8 km of coastal road separates you from the bay.

budva.title: Car Hire in Budva
budva.subtitle: Montenegro's riviera — 30 minutes from Kotor.
budva.seoDesc: Swap Kotor's stone lanes for Budva's beaches — 25 km south along the coast. Mogren Beach by day, Old Town nightlife after dark. A world apart in 30 minutes.

perast.title: Car Hire in Perast
perast.subtitle: A baroque gem 15 minutes from Kotor.
perast.seoDesc: Drive 15 minutes along the bay to Perast's baroque palaces. Hop on a boat to Our Lady of the Rocks — the island locals built stone by stone since 1452.

podgorica.title: Car Hire in Podgorica
podgorica.subtitle: Montenegro's capital — 90 minutes from Kotor.
podgorica.seoDesc: Take the dramatic Lovcen serpentine road from Kotor to the capital. 25 hairpin bends, Lake Skadar views, and 1.5 hours of Montenegro's wildest scenery.

podgorica-airport.title: Podgorica Airport Car Hire
podgorica-airport.subtitle: Montenegro's main international gateway — drive to Kotor in 90 minutes.
podgorica-airport.seoDesc: Flying budget airlines into TGD? Collect your car at Podgorica arrivals and reach Kotor's bay in 90 minutes via the 170 km inland route through the mountains.

dubrovnik-airport.seoDesc: Arriving via Dubrovnik? The 2-hour coastal drive to Kotor passes one border crossing. Green card and full insurance included — just show your passport and go.

tivat.title: Car Hire in Tivat
tivat.subtitle: Montenegro's marina town — 20 minutes from Kotor.
tivat.seoDesc: Porto Montenegro's superyachts are 20 minutes from Kotor's medieval walls. Grab your car in Tivat and contrast marina luxury with UNESCO heritage in one day.

montenegro.title: Montenegro Driving Guide
montenegro.subtitle: Everything you need to know about driving from Kotor across Montenegro.
montenegro.seoDesc: Narrow bay roads, mountain switchbacks, and Old Town parking decoded. Speed limits, fuel prices, and what cruise-ship visitors need to know before driving.

border-crossing.title: Balkans Border Crossing Guide
border-crossing.subtitle: Everything you need to know about driving your rental car across borders from Kotor.
border-crossing.seoDesc: Green card, vehicle papers, and passport — what you need to cross into Croatia, Bosnia, Albania, or Kosovo from Kotor. Wait times and queue tips included.

airportPickup.title: Tivat Airport Pickup
airportPickup.desc: Collect your car at Tivat Airport, just 8 km from Kotor. No shuttle, no waiting.

fullInsurance.title: Full Insurance
fullInsurance.desc: Comprehensive coverage with zero excess option. Drive the Bay of Kotor with peace of mind.

support.title: 24/7 Support
support.desc: Roadside assistance and customer service available around the clock, every day.

noHiddenFees.title: No Hidden Fees
noHiddenFees.desc: Fuel policy, mileage, tax — everything is included in the price you see.

freeCancellation.title: Free Cancellation
freeCancellation.desc: Plans change. Cancel up to 24 hours before pickup for a full refund.

crossBorder.title: Cross-Border
crossBorder.desc: Drive into Croatia, Bosnia, Albania, or Kosovo. Cross-border travel permitted.

0.text: Picked up a Polo at Tivat Airport and were in Kotor Old Town within 15 minutes. Spent the week driving to Perast, Budva, and up to Lovcen. Fantastic service.

1.text: The bay road from Kotor to Perast was the highlight of our holiday. Having our own car made it easy to stop at every viewpoint. Green card for Croatia was ready at pickup.

2.text: We drove from Kotor to Dubrovnik and back in a day, then explored Lovcen National Park the next. The Jeep handled the mountain roads with no issues at all.

0.a: A valid driving licence (must show Latin script details), a foreign passport, and a physical credit card in the main driver's name. International Driving Permit recommended if your licence is only valid in its country of origin. Copies or electronic licences are not accepted.

1.q: Can I drive to Croatia or Albania?
1.a: Yes! If you plan to travel outside Montenegro, notify the rental agent in advance so they can complete the required documents. Cross-border travel to neighbouring countries is permitted.

2.a: The minimum age and driving experience requirements vary by vehicle. These details are shown when you search for available cars.

3.a: All vehicles come with insurance — most include Third Party Liability and Collision Damage Waiver (CDW) in the base price. VAT, equipment kits (first aid, warning triangle, visibility vest), and a transparent fuel policy are also included. Mileage terms vary by vehicle. Extended coverage options are available for an additional fee.

4.q: How does pickup work at Tivat Airport?
4.a: Choose your pickup time during booking. The agent will meet you at Tivat Airport arrivals with your name on a sign. Your car is parked right outside. Total time from landing to driving into Kotor: about 15 minutes.

5.q: Can I drop off at a different location?
5.a: Yes — one-way rentals are supported across 28 cities and towns in Montenegro. Simply choose different pickup and dropoff locations during booking.

6.a: Free cancellation up to 7 days before the rental start date. For luxury cars and convertibles, free cancellation is available up to 30 days before. A 6% payment service fee applies to all cancellations.

7.a: Deposit amounts vary by vehicle — some cars are available with no deposit. If required, the deposit must be on a physical credit card in the main driver's name and is refunded upon return of the vehicle in good condition.

8.q: What happens if I exceed the mileage limit?
8.a: Vehicles with mileage restrictions are charged per kilometre over the limit. Rates are shown in the vehicle details before you book.

9.a: No. The price you see includes all taxes and fees. What's shown at booking is what you pay.

10.q: How much do I pay upfront?
10.a: Only 15–20% of the total is required to confirm your booking. The remainder is paid directly to the rental agent at pickup.

11.q: Can I rent without a credit card?
11.a: Some vehicles are available without a credit card. Check availability when searching — card-free options are clearly marked.

aboutBody.p1: Since 2007, Kotor Car Hire has been connecting travellers with reliable, affordable rental cars in the Bay of Kotor. What started as a simple idea — making it easier for visitors to explore this UNESCO-listed corner of the Adriatic — has grown into one of the region's most trusted car rental services.
aboutBody.p2: The Bay of Kotor is best experienced at your own pace. The winding bay road, hidden mountain villages, and medieval towns are difficult to reach by public transport. We saw an opportunity to give travellers the freedom to discover Kotor and its surroundings on their own terms — no tour bus schedules, no missed viewpoints, no compromises.
aboutBody.p3: We provide car rental services throughout the Bay of Kotor and Montenegro, with pickup and dropoff at Tivat Airport (8 km from Kotor), Kotor Old Town, and locations across the coast and interior. Our fleet ranges from compact city cars to spacious SUVs, all fully insured with no hidden fees.
aboutBody.promise1: Full insurance with zero excess options available
aboutBody.promise2: Tivat Airport meet-and-greet — we bring the car to you at arrivals
aboutBody.promise3: No hidden fees — the price you see is the price you pay
aboutBody.promise4: Cross-border travel permitted to Croatia, Bosnia, Albania, Serbia, and Kosovo
aboutBody.p4: Based in Kotor, we know every road around the bay. Whether you're driving from Tivat Airport to Kotor's UNESCO Old Town, taking the scenic bay road to Perast and Our Lady of the Rocks, heading south to Budva's beaches, or climbing the serpentine to Lovcen National Park, we can help you plan the perfect road trip.
aboutBody.p5: Have a question about renting a car in Kotor? We're available via phone, WhatsApp, and Viber. Search our fleet online and book in minutes — or reach out and we'll help you find the perfect car for your trip.

kotorBody.p1: Kotor is one of the best-preserved medieval towns on the Adriatic, sitting at the head of a dramatic fjord-like bay. The UNESCO-listed Old Town is a maze of narrow streets, Venetian palaces, Romanesque churches, and lively squares. Above it all, the fortress of San Giovanni climbs 1,200 steps to sweeping views across the bay.
kotorBody.p2: Renting a car in Kotor gives you the freedom to explore the entire Bay of Kotor and beyond. Tivat Airport is just 8 km away — a 15-minute drive. Parking is available outside the Old Town walls, with larger lots on the approach roads. In summer, arrive early to secure the best spots.
kotorBody.stJohnText: The fortifications above Kotor date back to the 6th century and were expanded by the Venetians. The 1,200-step climb to the top rewards with panoramic views of the bay, Old Town rooftops, and the surrounding mountains. Entry is free outside peak season.
kotorBody.cathedralText: Built in 809 AD and reconstructed in 1124, the Cathedral of St. Tryphon is one of the finest Romanesque buildings on the Adriatic coast. The twin bell towers are Kotor's most recognisable landmark, and the interior houses a collection of medieval gold and silverwork.
kotorBody.perastTrip: Perast is 15 minutes north along the bay road. From there, take a boat to Our Lady of the Rocks, the man-made island church that locals have been building with thrown rocks since 1452. The drive alone is one of the most scenic in Montenegro.
kotorBody.cetinjeTrip: The former royal capital of Cetinje is about an hour from Kotor via the serpentine road through Lovcen National Park. Visit the monastery that houses the hand of John the Baptist, then continue to the Njegos Mausoleum at 1,657 metres.
kotorBody.royalCourtText: Montenegro's royal palace from 1871 in Cetinje displays art, artefacts, and photographs from the country's royal history. Located next to the monastery in the former royal capital.

budvaBody.h1: Budva — The Heart of the Montenegrin Riviera
budvaBody.p1: Budva is Montenegro's most popular beach destination, just 30 minutes south of Kotor through a dramatic mountain tunnel. The town attracts visitors from across Europe to its stunning coastline, vibrant nightlife, and beautifully preserved medieval Old Town.
budvaBody.p2: With a rental car from Kotor, the entire Budva Riviera is at your fingertips. Budva sits roughly 20 minutes south of Tivat Airport and combines 2,500 years of history with a modern holiday atmosphere — ancient city walls by day, bustling beach bars by night.
budvaBody.beachesIntro: The Budva Riviera stretches for 21 kilometres with some of the finest beaches on the Adriatic:
budvaBody.mogren: Two connected pebble beaches reached through a tunnel cut into the cliffs, just minutes from the Old Town
budvaBody.jaz: A large sandy beach popular with locals and visitors alike, host to music festivals in summer
budvaBody.svetiStefan: The iconic islet-hotel visible from the coastal road, with a public beach on one side
budvaBody.becici: A long stretch of fine sand that was once voted the most beautiful beach in Europe
budvaBody.oldTownText: Budva's Old Town (Stari Grad) is a compact walled citadel on a small peninsula. Inside the walls you'll find narrow cobblestone streets, Venetian-era architecture, small churches dating back to the 7th century, and a lively mix of restaurants, galleries, and boutiques. The Citadela fortress at the southern tip offers panoramic coastal views.
budvaBody.svetiStefanTrip: Just 6 km south, this fortified island village is now a luxury hotel but the views from the road above are spectacular. The beach on the mainland side is open to all.
budvaBody.kotorTrip: A 30-minute drive north through the mountain tunnel brings you to the Bay of Kotor and its UNESCO-listed Old Town — the main reason many people hire a car in Budva.
budvaBody.lovcenTrip: Head inland for mountain scenery and the Njegos Mausoleum, perched at 1,657 metres with views across four countries on a clear day. The winding road from the coast is an experience in itself.
budvaBody.gettingAroundText: While Budva's Old Town is best explored on foot, a rental car is essential for reaching the beaches, nearby villages, and inland attractions. Parking is available near the Old Town walls and along the main road. In peak summer, arriving early secures the best spots.

perastBody.h1: Perast — Baroque Gem on the Bay
perastBody.p1: Perast is a jewel of a village just 15 minutes north of Kotor along the bay road. Despite its tiny size — home to only around 350 permanent residents — Perast punches well above its weight in history, architecture, and sheer beauty.
perastBody.p2: From 1420 to 1797, the town belonged to the Republic of Venice and was known for its master shipbuilders and sailors. Today, the waterfront is lined with elegant Baroque palaces, many now converted into small hotels and restaurants with bay-front terraces.
perastBody.stGeorgeText: A natural island just offshore, home to the St. George Benedictine Monastery dating from the 12th century. The island is closed to visitors but is beautifully photogenic from the harbour — a dark cypress-covered silhouette against the bay.
perastBody.ladyOfRocksText: A man-made island holding the church of the same name. Since 1452, locals have been throwing rocks into the bay to enlarge it. Every year on 22 July, the tradition continues as locals fill their boats with rocks. Ferry boats from Perast harbour cost a few euros and the crossing takes five minutes.
perastBody.historicText: Perast boasts 16 palaces, 17 Catholic churches, and several guard towers. The Museum of Perast in the old Palata Buiovich — widely considered the most beautiful building on the Montenegrin coast — houses artefacts, archives, paintings, and town records spanning centuries.
perastBody.monasteryText: Located just north of Perast on the road to Risan, the Banja Monastery features ancient religious pieces from Russian, Greek, and Boka dynasties. Continue north on the bay road for just a few kilometres to reach it.

tivatBody.h1: Tivat — Marina Town on the Bay
tivatBody.p1: Tivat sits just 20 minutes from Kotor on the opposite side of the bay. The town hosts Tivat Airport — the closest airport to Kotor at just 8 km — making it the natural arrival point for most visitors to the Bay of Kotor.
tivatBody.p2: Once a quiet naval base, Tivat has been transformed by the Porto Montenegro development into one of the Adriatic's most fashionable marina destinations. The seafront combines modern luxury with the relaxed pace of a small Montenegrin town.
tivatBody.beachesText: Tivat has 17 beaches spanning 3 kilometres of coastline. Top beaches include:
tivatBody.beach1: Plavi Horizanti (Blue Horizons): White sand and crystal clear waters, one of the bay's best swimming spots
tivatBody.beach2: Przno on Sveti Marko Island: An abandoned resort area offering seclusion and pristine waters
tivatBody.portoText: The former naval base has been converted into a luxury yacht marina and waterfront village. Porto Montenegro features designer boutiques, waterfront restaurants, a pool club, and regular events throughout the summer. It's become one of the Adriatic's premier superyacht destinations.
tivatBody.monasteryText: 10th-century ruins located on the Prevlaka peninsula, with ongoing archaeological work uncovering the site's ancient history.
tivatBody.churchText: Built in 1734, this church contains religious paintings and artefacts, with views over the bay towards Kotor.
tivatBody.museumText: Just a 20-minute drive away in Kotor, the Maritime Museum displays the bay's rich seafaring heritage spanning centuries of Venetian and Austro-Hungarian influence.

tivatAirportBody.p1: Tivat Airport is the closest airport to Kotor — just 8 km and a 15-minute drive to the UNESCO Old Town. The airport has undergone recent modernisation with a new terminal for international departures. The airport code is TIV, located just 4 km from Tivat town centre.
tivatAirportBody.p2: Tivat has more flights during the summer peak season but maintains daily connections to Belgrade year-round. Charter flights operate heavily during summer. The airport is well-known among pilots for its challenging approach between the mountains and the bay.
tivatAirportBody.facilitiesText: The terminal offers cafes, souvenir stores, and duty-free shopping. Bus services connect to Herceg Novi and Budva, with taxis available outside the terminal. However, having a rental car waiting is by far the most convenient option.
tivatAirportBody.whyText: Tivat Airport is the natural starting point for exploring the Bay of Kotor. Within 15 minutes of landing you can be driving through the gates of Kotor Old Town. Head north along the bay to Perast and Our Lady of the Rocks. Go south through the tunnel to Budva's beaches. Or climb the serpentine road to Lovcen National Park. No transfers, no waiting for buses.
tivatAirportBody.meetText: We meet you at the arrivals hall with your name on a sign. Your car is parked right outside. Total time from landing to driving into Kotor: about 15 minutes.

podgoricaBody.p1: Podgorica is Montenegro's capital and largest city, located about 90 minutes inland from Kotor via the scenic mountain road. The city sits at the confluence of six rivers — the Moraca, Ribnica, Zeta, Sitnica, Mareza, and Cijevna — and is home to approximately 170,000 residents.
podgoricaBody.p2: The city's architecture reflects centuries of diverse rule. Ottoman-era structures stand alongside communist-era functionalist buildings and contemporary glass towers. The Millennium Bridge has become a symbol of Podgorica's modern aesthetic.
podgoricaBody.p3: While most visitors to the Bay of Kotor head for the coast, Podgorica is worth a detour for those wanting to see another side of Montenegro — its commercial heart, cultural institutions, and gateway to the mountainous interior.
podgoricaBody.seeText: Notable attractions include the Ottoman-era Sat-Kula (clock tower), the archaeological site of Duklja (dating back two thousand years), and the modern Cathedral of the Resurrection. The city also has excellent restaurants, wine bars, and a growing arts scene.
podgoricaBody.skadarText: The largest lake in the Balkans, straddling the Montenegro-Albania border. A national park and home to millions of birds including pelicans, it's a must for nature lovers. The drive from Podgorica takes about 30 minutes.
podgoricaBody.danilovgradText: A scenic river town 30 minutes west of Podgorica, featuring riverside beaches, boat rides, galleries, and the nearby Zdrebaonik monastery.
podgoricaBody.zabljak: An alpine town in the Durmitor mountain region at 1,500 metres, 170 km from Podgorica. A year-round destination offering skiing in winter and hiking, rafting, and mountain biking in summer.

podgoricaAirportBody.p1: Podgorica Airport is Montenegro's largest international airport and the main hub for national carriers. While Tivat Airport is closer to Kotor (8 km vs 90 minutes), Podgorica often has better year-round flight connections, especially from Eastern European cities.
podgoricaAirportBody.p2: A new terminal opened in May 2006, while the old terminal now functions as a museum of Montenegro's aviation history. The airport code TGD comes from the city's former name, Titograd.
podgoricaAirportBody.distIntro: The airport is 12 km south of Podgorica city centre.
podgoricaAirportBody.dist2: Kotor: approximately 90 minutes via the mountain road
podgoricaAirportBody.flightsText: Daily service connects to Belgrade, with regular flights to Paris, Rome, Frankfurt, Vienna, Budapest, Ljubljana, and Moscow. Seasonal routes include Prague and additional services during summer.
podgoricaAirportBody.facilitiesText: The terminal features eight departure gates, two arrival gates, and 25,000 square metres of parking. Amenities include cafes, duty-free shopping, and car rental desks.
podgoricaAirportBody.awardText: Podgorica Airport has won the ACI Europe award for the best airport under 1 million passengers.
podgoricaAirportBody.rentalText: We meet you at the arrivals hall and bring the car to you. No queues, no shuttle buses. Within minutes of clearing customs, you can be on the road heading to Kotor and the Bay of Kotor — a scenic 90-minute drive through the mountains.

dubrovnikAirportBody.h1: Renting a Car at Dubrovnik Airport (DBV)
dubrovnikAirportBody.p1: Many visitors to Kotor fly into Dubrovnik Airport in Croatia and drive south into Montenegro. The airport has far more international connections than either Montenegrin airport, and Kotor is just a 2-hour drive down the spectacular coastal road.
dubrovnikAirportBody.p2: The route from Dubrovnik to Kotor follows the E65 along the Adriatic coast, crossing the Montenegro border at Debeli Brijeg. It's one of the most scenic coastal drives in Europe, passing through Herceg Novi and along the entire Bay of Kotor.
dubrovnikAirportBody.p3: Renting a car at Dubrovnik for a trip to Kotor is straightforward — just make sure your rental company provides a Green Card for cross-border insurance at booking.
dubrovnikAirportBody.borderText: During summer, the Debeli Brijeg crossing between Croatia and Montenegro can have queues of 1-2 hours. Cross early in the morning or late at night to avoid the worst delays. Your rental company should provide a Green Card for cross-border insurance — confirm this at booking.
dubrovnikAirportBody.facilitiesText1: Dubrovnik Airport has a modern terminal less than 30 minutes from Dubrovnik city on the E65. Parking is limited in high season — pre-book if possible. There is a discount for pre-booking.
dubrovnikAirportBody.facilitiesText2: Inside the terminal you'll find a bureau de change, bank, post office, restaurants, coffee shops, duty-free store, and newsstand.
dubrovnikAirportBody.gettingText1: The drive from Dubrovnik Airport to Kotor takes approximately 2 hours, depending on border wait times. The route follows the E65 coastal road south through Herceg Novi, then along the northern shore of the Bay of Kotor through Perast to Kotor.
dubrovnikAirportBody.gettingText2: It's one of Europe's most dramatic coastal drives. You'll pass through the Montenegro border, wind along the bay's edge with mountain walls rising on one side and the Adriatic on the other, and arrive at Kotor's medieval walls from the most stunning approach possible.

montenegroBody.h1: Exploring Montenegro by Car from Kotor
montenegroBody.p1: Kotor makes an ideal base for exploring Montenegro by car. The Bay of Kotor itself offers some of the most dramatic driving in Europe, and from here you can reach beaches, mountains, lakes, and historic towns within one to two hours.
montenegroBody.p2: Although compact in size, Montenegro's mountain switchbacks, narrow coastal roads, and scenic routes mean driving times are often longer than maps suggest. That's a feature, not a bug — the journeys are as memorable as the destinations.
montenegroBody.startText: Most visitors to Kotor pick up their rental car at Tivat Airport, just 8 km away. Podgorica Airport serves the capital and interior, while Dubrovnik Airport in Croatia is popular for its wider flight network. All three airports have car hire desks and meet-and-greet services.
montenegroBody.timesText1: Perast: 15 minutes. Tivat: 20 minutes. Budva: 30 minutes. Herceg Novi: 45 minutes. Cetinje: 60 minutes. Podgorica: 90 minutes. Dubrovnik: 2 hours. Zabljak (Durmitor): 4 hours.
montenegroBody.timesText2: Mountain roads add time but the scenery more than compensates. The serpentine from Kotor to Lovcen has 25 hairpin bends and some of the most dramatic views in the Balkans.
montenegroBody.rulesIntro: Traffic police checks are frequent in Montenegro. You'll need:
montenegroBody.rule1: A valid driver's licence (international licence accepted)
montenegroBody.rule2: Rental contract or proof of vehicle ownership
montenegroBody.rule4: A "Green Card" for border crossings (approximately 15 Euros for 15 days)
montenegroBody.keyRule1: Seat belts are mandatory for all passengers
montenegroBody.keyRule2: Mobile phone use while driving is prohibited
montenegroBody.keyRule3: Any drinking and driving is illegal — zero tolerance
montenegroBody.conditionsText: Roads around the Bay of Kotor are generally well-maintained but narrow in places, especially the bay road between Kotor and Perast. Mountain roads can be challenging with minimal guard rails. Drive cautiously on mountain passes and narrow coastal stretches.
montenegroBody.e65Text: The main route around the Bay of Kotor connects Herceg Novi to Budva via Kotor, Perast, and Tivat. This is the most popular driving route and offers stunning views of the bay at every turn.
montenegroBody.e762Text: Bisects the country from the coast to the interior, connecting the Adriatic to eastern Montenegro and Croatia. Takes you through Montenegro's mountainous heartland.
montenegroBody.borderText: Cross-border travel from Kotor is straightforward with the right documentation. The Croatia crossing at Debeli Brijeg can have long queues in summer. Weekday mornings or late evenings are the quietest times to cross.
montenegroBody.nationText: Montenegro achieved independence in 2006 and remains one of Europe's most underrated destinations. From the medieval walls of Kotor to the alpine peaks of Durmitor, the country packs extraordinary variety into a small area. Having your own car is the best way to experience it all.

borderCrossingBody.h1: Can I Take My Rental Car Across Borders?
borderCrossingBody.p1: Yes. Cross-border travel is permitted from Montenegro to Croatia, Bosnia & Herzegovina, Albania, Kosovo, and Serbia. Just let your rental company know at booking so they can prepare the necessary documentation.
borderCrossingBody.greenCardText: A Green Card is an international motor insurance certificate that proves your vehicle is insured in the country you're entering. It's required at every border crossing in the Balkans.
borderCrossingBody.greenCard2: Where to get it: your rental company arranges this — just confirm at booking
borderCrossingBody.greenCard3: Countries covered: Croatia, Bosnia, Albania, Kosovo, Serbia
borderCrossingBody.greenCardWarn: Without a Green Card, border guards will not let you through. Always check that the card covers all countries on your planned route before you set off.
borderCrossingBody.docsTitle: Documents You Need at the Border
borderCrossingBody.doc1: Valid passport or EU ID card
borderCrossingBody.doc5: Vehicle registration document (provided with the car)
borderCrossingBody.croatiaText: The most popular crossing for tourists heading from Kotor to Dubrovnik. The main checkpoint is at Debeli Brijeg on the coastal road (E65) between Herceg Novi and Dubrovnik. This crossing gets extremely busy in summer — expect queues of 1-2 hours in July and August.
borderCrossingBody.croatiaTip: Best times to cross: early morning (before 8am) or late evening (after 8pm). Weekday mornings are significantly quieter than weekends.
borderCrossingBody.croatiaFerry: The Kamenari-Lepetane ferry shortcut across the Bay of Kotor avoids driving around the entire bay and saves about 45 minutes when heading north from Kotor.
borderCrossingBody.albaniaText: Two main crossings: Sukobin/Muriqan (coastal, near Ulcinj) and Hani i Hotit (inland, near Skadar Lake). The coastal crossing is more traveller-friendly with shorter wait times.
borderCrossingBody.albaniaNote: Note: Albanian roads differ significantly from Montenegro. Drive cautiously, especially on rural roads.
borderCrossingBody.bosniaText: The main crossing is at Šćepan Polje (near Foča) if heading north, or via Vilusi from Nikšić. Crossings are generally quiet with minimal wait times outside summer.
borderCrossingBody.serbiaText: Cross at Dobrakovo (on the E65 north of Bijelo Polje). This crossing connects directly to the Serbian motorway system towards Belgrade.
borderCrossingBody.kosovoText: The crossing at Kula (near Rožaje) connects to Peja/Peć in Kosovo. Check with your rental company that Kosovo is included on your Green Card — some policies exclude it.
borderCrossingBody.tip1: Have all documents ready before you reach the checkpoint — don't search for them at the window
borderCrossingBody.tip2: Turn off your engine while waiting in queues (border guards appreciate it)
borderCrossingBody.tip3: Summer weekends are the worst — plan to cross on weekdays if possible
borderCrossingBody.tip4: The Croatian coastal crossing (Debeli Brijeg) is busiest; inland crossings are quieter
borderCrossingBody.tip5: Keep some cash in Euros — useful for tolls and minor purchases on the other side
borderCrossingBody.tip6: Mobile data roaming: check your plan covers the country you're entering
borderCrossingBody.lawsTitle: Driving Rules That Change at Borders
borderCrossingBody.lawsIntro: Each country has slightly different traffic laws. Key differences:
borderCrossingBody.law1: Croatia: headlights must be on at all times. Motorway tolls apply (cash or card)
borderCrossingBody.law2: Albania: speed limits are lower on rural roads. Road quality varies significantly
borderCrossingBody.law3: Bosnia: winter tyres mandatory November-April. Carry a first-aid kit and warning triangle
borderCrossingBody.law4: Serbia: zero alcohol tolerance for drivers. Motorway vignette required
borderCrossingBody.law5: Kosovo: drive on the right. International driving permit recommended
borderCrossingBody.route1: Kotor → Dubrovnik: 2 hours via coastal E65. Stunning scenery, one border crossing
borderCrossingBody.route2: Kotor → Mostar (Bosnia): 3-4 hours via Trebinje. Visit the famous bridge
borderCrossingBody.route3: Kotor → Shkodër (Albania): 3 hours via Podgorica and Skadar Lake
borderCrossingBody.route4: Kotor → Belgrade (Serbia): 6-7 hours via Podgorica and E65 north
borderCrossingBody.ctaText: Need a car with cross-border documentation ready? Search our fleet — just select your destination countries at booking and we'll arrange the Green Card.
